title: Europe watches the next American revolution take shape
gist: A former US diplomat now at Chatham House argues the US is undergoing a durable, cross-partisan inward turn (irrespective of who wins the 2026 midterms or succeeds Trump), and Europe must adapt to a narrower, more transactional US-Europe relationship rather than expect a post-Trump restoration.

themes.theme: The US inward turn is structural, not just Trump

insights.claim: The author argues the current wave of US political discontent is not a short-lived gust but a sustained gale that will persist irrespective of who controls Congress or the White House next.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Investors and allies should plan for a multi-year, not single-administration, shift in US global posture rather than betting on reversion after Trump leaves office.

insights.claim: Americans' top worries heading into the debate are healthcare availability and affordability, followed by the economy, inflation, federal spending/deficit, and income and wealth distribution.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Domestic economic anxiety (not foreign policy) is the primary driver of the political realignment, meaning foreign policy retrenchment is a downstream effect of domestic priorities.

insights.claim: Proposed remedies to US discontent cut across party lines and include reining in military commitments abroad to redirect spending home, dismantling the 'billionaire class' for economic justice, and consolidating executive power to unleash presidential authority.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: These competing remedies (retrenchment, redistribution, executive consolidation) are live debates ahead of the November midterms and will shape whether US retrenchment is fiscal, populist, or authoritarian in character.
insights.locator: paragraph on 'sharp division on the remedies'

insights.claim: The author frames today's US political turmoil as an echo of the spirit of the American Revolution 250 years ago, but this time directed inward at the current system's failures rather than at an external power.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A rhetorical framing device signaling the author views the change as foundational/generational rather than a normal political cycle.
insights.quote: History doesn’t repeat itself, but in the US its echoes carry a similar spirit of revolt.

insights.claim: Trump did not create the nativist and anti-alliance backlash but stoked pre-existing fears rooted in decades of trade liberalization that brought cheap goods while closing US factories, and immigration that fueled both innovation and nativist backlash.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Positions Trump as an accelerant of pre-existing structural economic grievances rather than their root cause, implying the trend outlasts him.

themes.theme: Durable features of the new US global posture

insights.claim: The author predicts the current era of Washington prioritizing American manufacturing and higher trade barriers, greater burden-sharing demands on allies, a more limited global US defence role, and presidential transactionalism will all outlast Trump's presidency.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Core actionable forecast: expect continued US tariff/trade barrier policy and reduced US defense commitments regardless of the 2028 election outcome, informing long-horizon allocation to European defense and manufacturing plays.
insights.quote: Today’s era, of a Washington that prioritizes American manufacturing and higher trade barriers, is here to stay.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'Whoever wins, Europe is right to anticipate'

insights.claim: Despite retrenchment, the US will still need Europe for global financial markets, emerging technology cooperation, intelligence sharing, select military cooperation, and alignment on China.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The alliance narrows in scope but persists in specific high-value domains (finance, tech, intel) — these are the areas where transatlantic cooperation remains investable.
insights.locator: paragraph on post-WWII structure / repeated in pull-quote

themes.theme: Europe's defensive hedging and the transatlantic bargain

insights.claim: Fear of US dependency has accelerated European investment in NATO spending, an independent defence industrial base delinked from US defence trade, sovereign AI capabilities, and space/satellite infrastructure, which the author believes can become sources of long-run transatlantic strength.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Direct investable theme: European defense industrial base, sovereign AI, and space/satellite infrastructure are structurally favored by this dynamic.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'Europe needs the US too'

insights.claim: A former US official told the author they were pessimistic about restoring US-Europe trust post-Trump, using the metaphor that loveless marriages survive because one spouse keeps cutting the grass and the other keeps cooking dinner.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Captures the author's core thesis in a memorable frame: the alliance persists on mutual utility, not affection or shared values.
insights.quote: many loveless marriages survive because one spouse keeps cutting the grass and the other keeps cooking dinner
insights.locator: anecdote about hosting a former US official

insights.claim: The proposed transatlantic bargain going forward: the US provides Europe a nuclear umbrella, integration in US capital markets, and preferential tech access; Europe grants the US access to its capital, secure supply chains for key technology inputs, and extends the reach of US sanctions.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A concrete transactional framework investors can use to judge which sectors (capital markets, tech supply chains, sanctions enforcement) remain load-bearing in the relationship even as broader alignment erodes.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'In my view, it goes something like this'

insights.claim: As Europe takes fuller ownership of security in its own neighborhood, especially its eastern flank, this frees the US to reorient its strategic focus toward Asia/China.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Confirms the mechanism by which European rearmament directly enables US pivot to China, a trend with implications for defense spending allocation and US-China competition dynamics.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'As Europe takes on fuller ownership'

insights.claim: The shared language of democracy and universal values no longer centers the US-Europe relationship, which the author describes as 'bound together, joyless yet committed.'
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Signals a values-based alliance narrative is being replaced by a purely transactional one, a lens for interpreting future US-Europe disputes as economic/security bargaining rather than value alignment.
insights.quote: the US and Europe remain bound together, joyless yet committed
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'In my view, it goes something like this'

insights.claim: The author (a former US diplomat now at Chatham House, located in a building rumored to be near where King George III plotted against American revolutionaries) uses the 250-year-old parallel to argue Europe cannot control America's current reinvention any more than Britain could stop the original revolution.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Sets the article's central takeaway: European strategy should shift from trying to influence US direction to adapting to it, since attempts at control historically fail.
insights.locator: opening paragraphs on King George III
